skill.name: ssh-penetration-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized SSH security assessments, including service discovery, configuration review, credential testing, tunneling, exploitation research, and post-access checks.
skill.summary: Assess SSH services and workflows for authorized security testing.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are largely confirmed in context: the file is an offensive SSH penetration testing playbook with brute-force credential attacks, Metasploit modules, tunneling for pivoting, and post-exploitation persistence. The bash code-fence detections are not runtime execution by the skill, but the runnable instructions create high misuse risk. No evidence found of prompt injection text, but the skill should not be published in its current form.

critical_findings.title: Credential Attack Workflows
critical_findings.description: The skill provides direct Hydra, Medusa, Metasploit, and Paramiko workflows for SSH brute force, password spraying, and login attempts. These instructions can enable unauthorized access against real SSH services.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and script are explicitly labeled as brute-force or spraying workflows. The context confirms credential attack intent, not incidental tool usage.

critical_findings.title: Post-Exploitation Persistence and Credential Harvesting
critical_findings.description: The skill instructs users to find SSH keys, read SSH authorization files, inspect shell history for passwords, enumerate users, and add a key to authorized_keys for persistence after access is gained.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section is titled post-exploitation and includes explicit persistence and credential discovery steps. This is confirmed malicious-capable behavior in context.

critical_findings.title: Offensive Exploitation Tooling
critical_findings.description: The skill requires Metasploit and gives SSH scanner, username enumeration, login, and public-key login module workflows. This increases capability for unauthorized exploitation and credential compromise.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: Metasploit usage is explicit and repeated across prerequisites, exploitation, and quick reference sections. The surrounding workflow includes username enumeration and login attacks.

high_findings.title: SSH Tunneling for Pivoting and Reverse Callbacks
high_findings.description: The skill documents local, remote, and dynamic port forwarding, including SOCKS proxy use with proxychains and a reverse shell callback example. These techniques can support lateral movement.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tunneling examples are explicit and include network pivoting language and proxychains usage. Legitimate administration is possible, but the context is offensive testing.

high_findings.title: Private Key Discovery and Use
high_findings.description: The skill lists private key locations, suggests retrieving web-accessible private keys, and shows login attempts with discovered keys. Mishandling these steps can expose or misuse credentials.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file names and commands directly reference SSH private keys and login with key files. The semantic context is testing found or exposed keys.

high_findings.title: Network Scanning Against SSH Targets
high_findings.description: The skill provides Nmap, nc, telnet, ssh-audit, and Metasploit commands for discovering and enumerating SSH services across hosts and networks.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct network reconnaissance instructions. Scanning can be authorized, but paired with attack and post-exploitation phases it raises publication risk.

medium_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Guidance Requires Defensive Framing
medium_findings.description: The skill identifies weak SSH algorithms and ciphers, which is useful for defense. In this playbook it appears alongside exploit research and credential attacks, so it needs strict authorization framing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Weak algorithm identification is legitimate in audits. The risk comes from placement inside a broader offensive workflow.

medium_findings.title: Custom Paramiko Automation Can Execute Remote Commands
medium_findings.description: The Python example connects to SSH, prints attempted credentials, and runs remote commands after a successful login. This can automate unauthorized access if used outside approved scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly performs login attempts and command execution. It is shown as an example, but it materially increases misuse capability.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Runtime Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution at many code-fence boundaries. These are markdown examples and are not executed by the skill file itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are markdown fences or inline command examples, not executable Ruby source. The commands remain risky as documentation.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Hosts Are Mostly Illustrative Examples
low_findings.description: The fixed IP addresses and target.com URLs appear to be sample targets rather than hidden exfiltration endpoints. They still make unsafe commands easy to adapt.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses are common private-network examples and placeholder domains. I found no evidence that they are command-and-control or exfiltration infrastructure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Brute Force and Password Spraying Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Repeated examples use Hydra, Medusa, Ncrack, Metasploit, and Python automation for SSH credential attacks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and repeated across multiple tools. It is central to the skill purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistence Through authorized_keys
dangerous_patterns.description: The post-exploitation section instructs users to add their key to authorized_keys, which can create persistent unauthorized access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The comment names persistence and the command appends a key to authorized_keys. The risk is direct and unambiguous.

dangerous_patterns.title: SOCKS Proxy Pivoting
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill describes dynamic SSH forwarding and proxychains scanning through the tunnel, which can enable internal network pivoting.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section names SOCKS proxy use and network pivoting. The proxychains Nmap example confirms offensive network traversal capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: System and Credential File Reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to search for SSH key files, read SSH authorization data, inspect passwd, and search shell history for passwords.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly target sensitive local files and credential traces. They appear under post-exploitation activities.

content.value_statement: SSH assessments need clear scope, repeatable checks, and careful documentation. This skill organizes SSH discovery, configuration review, credential testing, tunneling analysis, and reporting for authorized work.
actual_capabilities: Outlines SSH service discovery and version enumeration steps.
Lists weak SSH algorithms, ciphers, MACs, and protocol issues to review.
Documents credential testing workflows using Hydra, Medusa, Metasploit, and Paramiko.
Explains SSH local, remote, dynamic forwarding, and jump host concepts.
Covers post-access checks for SSH keys, user data, configuration, and history files.
Provides quick-reference tables for enumeration, brute-force tools, and forwarding modes.
limitations: The skill assumes explicit written authorization for every target and technique.
It includes offensive instructions that can enable unauthorized access if misused.
It does not enforce scope, rate limits, logging, or approval checks.
It does not provide a remediation-only workflow or automated hardening plan.

use_cases.title: Review SSH Assessment Scope
use_cases.description: Map planned SSH testing activities to authorization, target ranges, allowed tools, and reporting expectations.

use_cases.title: Plan Authorized SSH Testing
use_cases.description: Structure discovery, configuration review, credential testing, tunneling checks, and evidence collection for an approved engagement.

use_cases.title: Prioritize SSH Hardening
use_cases.description: Translate assessment themes into defensive actions for algorithms, authentication methods, exposed keys, and forwarding controls.

prompt_templates.title: Define Safe Scope
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for an authorized review
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define a written scope for an authorized SSH security review. Include targets, allowed methods, timing, evidence handling, and stop conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Audit Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an SSH configuration audit checklist for approved systems. Focus on protocol versions, algorithms, authentication, logging, forwarding, and key management.

prompt_templates.title: Review Findings Defensively
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these SSH assessment findings and convert them into prioritized remediation steps. Avoid exploit instructions and include owner, impact, and validation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Engagement Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft an executive and technical report for an authorized SSH assessment. Include scope, methodology, confirmed risks, evidence summary, remediation, and retest criteria.

output_examples.input: Create a safe SSH review checklist for production Linux servers.
output: Scope and approvals: Confirm written authorization, target hosts, time window, and escalation contacts.
Configuration review: Check authentication policy, root login, forwarding controls, logging, and approved algorithms.
Evidence handling: Record findings without collecting private keys or password material.
Remediation plan: Prioritize exposed keys, weak algorithms, missing MFA, and excessive forwarding permissions.

output_examples.input: Summarize SSH findings for an infrastructure owner.
output: High priority: Disable weak algorithms and remove unused keys from authorized access paths.
Medium priority: Restrict port forwarding and require stronger authentication for administrators.
Validation: Retest configuration, confirm log coverage, and document exception owners.

output_examples.input: Prepare a report outline for an authorized SSH assessment.
output: Executive summary with business impact and remediation timeline.
Methodology covering discovery, configuration review, authentication review, and evidence limits.
Findings grouped by severity with affected hosts and defensive recommendations.

best_practices: Use the skill only for systems covered by written authorization and a defined scope.
Convert offensive examples into defensive checks and remediation tasks whenever possible.
Avoid collecting private keys, passwords, or shell history unless the engagement explicitly permits it.
anti_patterns: Do not use the credential attack steps against third-party or unknown systems.
Do not add SSH keys, create persistence, or pivot through networks without explicit approval.
Do not publish raw private keys, passwords, user lists, or sensitive host data in reports.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish as written?
faq.answer: No. It includes credential attacks, exploitation tooling, pivoting, and post-exploitation persistence instructions.

faq.question: Can the skill support legitimate security work?
faq.answer: Yes, but only under written authorization and with strict scope, logging, and evidence handling controls.

faq.question: Does it execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. The file is documentation, but it contains runnable commands and scripts that users could copy.

faq.question: What should be changed before publication?
faq.answer: Remove brute-force, persistence, pivoting, and exploit workflows. Reframe the content around hardening and defensive validation.

faq.question: Does the skill contain prompt injection text?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the reviewed SKILL.md file.

faq.question: Which tools does the skill reference?
faq.answer: It references Nmap, Hydra, Medusa, ssh-audit, Metasploit, proxychains, OpenSSH, and Paramiko.
